edges.description: Skin infiltration produces eczema-like and urticarial inflammation.
Gastrointestinal infiltration produces inflammatory digestive symptoms.
Airway infiltration promotes asthma-like respiratory disease.
Eosinophilic tissue injury can also involve the heart and pericardium.
Cardiac eosinophilic inflammation can involve the pericardium.
Eosinophil-driven hypercoagulability produces clinically important thrombosis.
Cutaneous eosinophilic inflammation can manifest as urticaria.
Cutaneous eosinophilic inflammation can manifest as eczema-like dermatitis.
Gastrointestinal eosinophilic inflammation can manifest as diarrhea.
Airway eosinophilic inflammation can manifest as asthma.

nodes.description: In lymphocytic HES, aberrant T-cell populations produce eosinophilopoietic cytokines, especially IL-5, which prolong eosinophil survival and expand the reactive eosinophil compartment.

evidence.reference_title: Hypereosinophilic syndrome: a multicenter, retrospective analysis of clinical characteristics and response to therapy.
evidence.snippet: and 29 of 168 patients tested (17%) had a demonstrable aberrant or clonal T-cell population.

nodes.description: Eosinophilic airway inflammation contributes to asthma-like respiratory manifestations in clinically active HES.

nodes.description: Targeted monoclonal antibodies against the IL-5 axis are used for severe or relapsing disease and are especially relevant when eosinophilia remains corticosteroid-dependent or corticosteroid-refractory.

evidence.snippet: Mepolizumab is a humanized monoclonal antibody. In conditions where eosinophilia is considered to play an important part in the pathology, including eosinophilic asthma, HES, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, a consistent reduction in blood eosinophil counts is observed in association with mepolizumab administration, with concomitant clinical improvement.

This is a multicentre, randomised, double-blind (DB), parallel-group, placebo-controlled, 24-week Phase III study to compare the efficacy and safety of benralizumab versus placebo administered by SC injection Q4W in patients with hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES).

nodes.description: Eosinophil-mediated tissue injury can extend to the heart, where chronic eosinophilic inflammation is surveilled for endomyocardial fibrosis, pericardial inflammation, and valvular damage.

nodes.description: Systemic glucocorticoids remain first-line therapy for many patients because they rapidly suppress eosinophilia and clinical manifestations.

evidence.snippet: Corticosteroid monotherapy induced complete or partial responses at 1 month in 85% (120/141) of patients with most remaining on maintenance doses (median, 10 mg prednisone equivalent daily for 2 months to 20 years).

evidence.snippet: Imatinib (used in 68 patients) was more effective in patients with the FIP1L1-PDGFRA mutation (88%) than in those without (23%; P < .001).

evidence.reference_title: Hypereosinophilic syndrome: a multicenter, retrospective analysis of clinical characteristics and response to therapy.
evidence.snippet: BACKGROUND: Hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) is a heterogeneous group of rare disorders defined by persistent blood eosinophilia > or =1.5 x 10(9)/L, absence of a secondary cause, and evidence of eosinophil-associated pathology.

nodes.description: A subset of HES is initiated by clonal myeloid lesions such as FIP1L1-PDGFRA rearrangement, which drive autonomous eosinophilopoiesis and define the primary myeloid end of the HES spectrum.

evidence.reference_title: Hypereosinophilic syndrome: a multicenter, retrospective analysis of clinical characteristics and response to therapy.
evidence.snippet: BACKGROUND: Hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) is a heterogeneous group of rare disorders defined by persistent blood eosinophilia > or =1.5 x 10(9)/L, absence of a secondary cause, and evidence of eosinophil-associated pathology.

nodes.description: Eosinophil granule proteins and protease systems can directly activate clotting pathways, inhibit endogenous anticoagulant mechanisms, and promote thromboembolic complications. This thromboinflammatory branch is clinically important because thrombosis is a frequent complication and can dominate long-term morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic Hypereosinophilia and Venous Thromboembolism: Is There a Pathophysiological or Clinical Link? Description of an Intriguing Clinical Case.
evidence.snippet: The proteolytic actions induced by eosinophil granules may activate the kallikrein system and so the clotting cascade by factor XII activation; the factor XII activation, increasing the activation of other proteases of clotting system, induces hypercoagulable state.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic Hypereosinophilia and Venous Thromboembolism: Is There a Pathophysiological or Clinical Link? Description of an Intriguing Clinical Case.
evidence.snippet: Furthermore, the eosinophil activation and degranulation increases the release of eosinophil cationic protein that is able to bind heparin and modulate thrombomodulin so reducing the physiological anticoagulation mechanisms and so increasing the prothrombotic state in hypereosinophilia.

evidence.reference_title: Idiopathic Hypereosinophilia and Venous Thromboembolism: Is There a Pathophysiological or Clinical Link? Description of an Intriguing Clinical Case.
evidence.snippet: Venous thromboembolism is the one of the most commonly described thromboses in hypereosinophilia and it may influence long-term treatment and prognosis of hypereosinophilic patients.

nodes.description: Activated eosinophils infiltrate organs and release cytotoxic granule proteins, amplifying local inflammation and directly injuring target tissues.
